---
title: "Homework 3"
author: "Mengyan Jing and Abhisek Banerjee"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: pdf_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)
set.seed(2026)
```

## Problem 1

We have a dataset given to us as peru.txt that consists of variables possibly relating to blood pressures of n = 39 Peruvians who have moved from rural high-altitude areas to urban lower- altitude areas.

Our goal is to construct a coordinate descent algorithm to find parameter estimates for a lasso (L2) regression of systolic blood pressure on the predictors $X_1-X_9$ ($X_1$ and $X_9$ are defined in the question).


### (1) Pseudocode

We have the lasso problem 
$$
\min_{\boldsymbol\beta \in \mathbb{R}^{p}}\;
\tfrac{1}{2}\,\big\|\mathbf{y} - X\boldsymbol\beta\big\|_{2}^{2}
\;+\; \lambda\,\|\boldsymbol\beta\|_{1}.
$$
The non-smooth part $\|\boldsymbol\beta\|_{1} = \sum_{i=1}^{p}|\beta_{i}|$
is separable, so coordinate descent should converge to a
global minimizer. Fixing all $\beta_{j}$, $j\neq i$, the
stationarity condition for $\beta_{i}$ is
$$
0 \;=\; X_{i}^{\top}X_{i}\,\beta_{i} \;+\; X_{i}^{\top}\!\big(X_{-i}\boldsymbol\beta_{-i} - \mathbf{y}\big) \;+\; \lambda\,s_{i},
\qquad s_{i}\in\partial|\beta_{i}|,
$$
which is solved in closed form by soft-thresholding (from the slides of Ryan Tibshirani of Carneige Mellon University):
$$
\beta_{i} \;\leftarrow\;
S_{\lambda/\|X_{i}\|_{2}^{2}}\!\left(
\frac{X_{i}^{\top}\!\big(\mathbf{y}-X_{-i}\boldsymbol\beta_{-i}\big)}
     {X_{i}^{\top}X_{i}}
\right),
\qquad
S_{\tau}(z)=\operatorname{sign}(z)\big(|z|-\tau\big)_{+}.
$$
Maintaining the full residual $\mathbf{r}=\mathbf{y}-X\boldsymbol\beta$,
the update can be rewritten as
$$
\beta_{i} \;\leftarrow\;
S_{\lambda/\|X_{i}\|_{2}^{2}}\!\left(
\frac{X_{i}^{\top}\mathbf{r}}{\|X_{i}\|_{2}^{2}} \;+\; \beta_{i}
\right),
$$
so each coordinate update costs $O(n)$ and one full cycle costs $O(np)$,
matching the cost of one gradient-descent step. Following from the above theory, the pseudo code for our coordinate descent algorithm is as follows:

```text
Inputs: Response variable  y  (in R)
        Design matrix    X  (in R^{n x p}, having columns X_1, ..., X_p)
        Penalty hyperparameter   lambda > 0
        A tolerance limit      (e.g. 1e-7)
        Number of cycles (maximum) K_max

Step 1. We can center y and each column of X (in this may the intercept will drop out)
    so... y    <- y - mean(y)
          X_i  <- X_i - mean(X_i),     i = 1, ..., p

Step 2. Compute column norms from beforehand (which is to be used in every update)
    c_i  <- ||X_i||_2^2 = X_i' X_i,   i = 1, ..., p

Step 3. Now we Initialize everything as zero 
    beta <- (0, 0, ..., 0)            # p-vector of zeros
    r    <- y                         # since beta = 0
    k    <- 0

Step 4. Cyclic coordinate descent
    repeat
        beta_old <- beta
        for i = 1, 2, ..., p do
            # 1. Add coordinate i back into the residual
            r <- r + X_i * beta[i]

            # 2. Univariate OLS coefficient on the partial residual
            z_i <- (X_i' r) / c_i

            # 3. Soft-thresholding update (slide 13)
            beta[i] <- sign(z_i) * max( |z_i| - lambda / c_i , 0 )

            # 4. Subtract the new contribution back out of the residual
            r = K_max

Step 5. Recover the intercept on the original (uncentered) scale
    beta_0 <- mean(y_orig) - sum_i ( beta[i] * mean(X_i_orig) )

Output: (beta_0, beta_1, ..., beta_p)
```



## Problem 2

### Problem Setup

We estimate $\theta \ge 0$ in the density
$$
f(x;\theta) = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{4\pi}}\,\frac{\exp(-\tfrac{1}{4x}-\theta x)}{x^{3/2}\exp(-\sqrt\theta)},\qquad x>0,
$$
from 1000 test datasets, each containing $k=50$ i.i.d. observations. This is
the inverse-Gaussian family $\text{IG}(\mu=\tfrac{1}{2\sqrt\theta},\lambda=\tfrac12)$
for $\theta>0$, reducing to the Lévy distribution at $\theta=0$.
Methods are scored by weighted MSE with $w(\theta)=1/(\theta+0.001)$,
which puts enormous weight on errors near the boundary $\theta\approx 0$.


#### Part 1 — Maximum Likelihood Estimation

**Log-likelihood.** For $\mathbf x=(x_1,\dots,x_k)$,
$$
\ell(\theta) = C(\mathbf x)-\theta\sum_{i=1}^k x_i + k\sqrt\theta,
$$
where $C(\mathbf x)=-\tfrac{k}{2}\log(4\pi)-\tfrac32\sum\log x_i-\tfrac14\sum 1/x_i$
does not depend on $\theta$.

**Optimization.** Setting $\ell'(\theta)=-\sum x_i+\tfrac{k}{2\sqrt\theta}=0$
gives the closed form
$$
\hat\theta_{\text{MLE}} = \left(\frac{k}{2\sum x_i}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{(2\bar x)^2},
$$
with $\ell''(\theta)=-k/(4\theta^{3/2})<0$ confirming a maximum. No
numerical optimizer is needed.

**Results.** Across the 1000 test datasets the MLE has median $0.0162$,
mean $0.3515$, and maximum $3.22$. The distribution is heavily concentrated
near zero, consistent with the prof's description of the test set.

```{r mle}
load("test_data.RData")
mle_theta <- function(x) (1 / (2 * mean(x)))^2
theta_mle <- sapply(Z_test, mle_theta)
summary(theta_mle)
```

#### Part 2 — Neural Bayes Estimator

**Training data simulation.** For each simulated $\theta$, we draw $k=50$
observations from $f(\cdot;\theta)$ using Michael–Schucany–Haas sampling
from $\text{IG}(1/(2\sqrt\theta),1/2)$ when $\theta>0$, and the exact Lévy
transform $X=1/(4Z^2),\,Z\sim N(0,1)$ when $\theta=0$.

**Training prior.** A boundary-heavy mixture chosen to match the test
distribution: 20% exact zeros, 50% $\text{Exp}(\text{rate}=5)$ (mean $0.2$),
30% $\text{Unif}(0,2.5)$. Exact zeros let the network learn to predict
$\theta=0$ explicitly.

**Architecture.** A DeepSets network with mean pooling over the 50
observations. Each observation is enriched to a 3-dimensional feature
vector $[x_i, 1/x_i, \log x_i]$, so the inner network directly sees the
sufficient-statistic ingredients. Six expert summary statistics
$(\hat\theta_{\text{MLE}}, \min x, \text{med}(x), \text{sd}(\log x),
\text{mean}(1/x), x_{(0.10)})$ are concatenated with the pooled neural
summary before entering the outer network. Hidden layers have width 128,
and the final layer uses `softplus` to enforce $\hat\theta\ge 0$.
Trained as a 3-model ensemble (one member discarded due to divergence —
see below).

**Loss.** We use a **custom weighted squared-error loss**
$L(\hat\theta,\theta) = (\hat\theta-\theta)^2/(\theta+0.001)$,
exactly matching the evaluation metric. Adam optimizer with learning
rate $10^{-3}$, batch size 256, 100 epochs, 50 steps per epoch, with
fresh batches simulated on-the-fly every step.

**Performance assessment.** Validation wMSE is computed on a fixed
held-out set of 2000 simulated datasets every 10 epochs. Final NBE
estimates are the arithmetic mean over surviving ensemble members,
clamped to $[0,\infty)$.

```{r torch-setup}
library(torch)
device <- if (cuda_is_available()) torch_device("cuda") else torch_device("cpu")
cat("Using device:", as.character(device), "\n")
```

```{r sampler}
rinvgauss_vec <- function(n, mu, lambda) {
  nu <- rnorm(n); y <- nu^2
  x  <- mu + (mu^2 * y) / (2 * lambda) -
    (mu / (2 * lambda)) * sqrt(4 * mu * lambda * y + mu^2 * y^2)
  ifelse(runif(n) <= mu / (mu + x), x, mu^2 / x)
}

sim_one_dataset <- function(theta, k = 50) {
  if (theta < 1e-12) {
    z <- rnorm(k); 1 / (4 * z^2)              # Levy
  } else {
    rinvgauss_vec(k, mu = 1/(2*sqrt(theta)), lambda = 0.5)
  }
}

sample_prior <- function(K) {
  th <- numeric(K)
  for (i in 1:K) {
    u <- runif(1)
    if (u < 0.20)      th[i] <- 0
    else if (u < 0.70) th[i] <- rexp(1, 5)
    else               th[i] <- runif(1, 0, 2.5)
  }
  th
}
```


```{r batch}
gen_batch <- function(B, k = 50) {
  thetas <- sample_prior(B)
  X <- matrix(NA, B, k); ES <- matrix(NA, B, 6)
  for (i in 1:B) {
    x <- sim_one_dataset(thetas[i], k); X[i, ] <- x
    ES[i, ] <- c(
      (1/(2*mean(x)))^2,        # MLE
      min(x), median(x),
      sd(log(x)), mean(1/x),
      quantile(x, 0.10, names = FALSE)
    )
  }
  # Enriched features [x, 1/x, log x] -> (B, k, 3)
  feat <- array(0, dim = c(B, k, 3))
  feat[,,1] <- X; feat[,,2] <- 1/X; feat[,,3] <- log(X)
  list(
    X     = torch_tensor(feat, dtype = torch_float()),
    S     = torch_tensor(ES,   dtype = torch_float()),
    theta = torch_tensor(matrix(thetas, ncol=1), dtype = torch_float())
  )
}
```


```{r model}
deepset <- nn_module(
  initialize = function(d_in = 3, d_expert = 6, w = 128) {
    self$psi <- nn_sequential(
      nn_linear(d_in, w), nn_relu(),
      nn_linear(w, w),    nn_relu(),
      nn_linear(w, w),    nn_relu()
    )
    self$phi <- nn_sequential(
      nn_linear(w + d_expert, w), nn_relu(),
      nn_linear(w, w),            nn_relu(),
      nn_linear(w, w),            nn_relu(),
      nn_linear(w, 1),            nn_softplus()
    )
  },
  forward = function(X, S) {
    h <- self$psi(X)$mean(dim = 2)            # mean pool over k
    self$phi(torch_cat(list(h, S), dim = 2))
  }
)

weighted_loss <- function(pred, true) {
  w <- 1 / (true + 0.001)
  (w * (pred - true)^2)$mean()
}
```

```{r train-one}
train_one <- function(seed, K = 20000, epochs = 100, batch = 256, k = 50) {
  torch_manual_seed(seed); set.seed(seed)
  model <- deepset()$to(device = device)
  opt   <- optim_adam(model$parameters, lr = 1e-3)

  val   <- gen_batch(2000, k)
  vX <- val$X$to(device=device); vS <- val$S$to(device=device); vT <- val$theta$to(device=device)

  for (e in 1:epochs) {
    model$train()
    for (s in 1:50) {                          # 50 steps per epoch
      b <- gen_batch(batch, k)
      bX <- b$X$to(device=device); bS <- b$S$to(device=device); bT <- b$theta$to(device=device)
      opt$zero_grad()
      loss <- weighted_loss(model(bX, bS), bT)
      loss$backward()
      opt$step()
    }
    if (e %% 10 == 0) {
      model$eval()
      with_no_grad({
        vl <- weighted_loss(model(vX, vS), vT)$item()
      })
      cat(sprintf("seed %d epoch %3d  val wMSE = %.4f\n", seed, e, vl))
    }
  }
  model
}
```


```{r ensemble}
models <- list()
for (i in 1:3) {
  cat("\n=== Training member", i, "===\n")
  models[[i]] <- train_one(seed = 2026 + i*100)
}
```

```{r predict}
# Build test tensors with enriched features + expert stats
B <- length(Z_test); k <- 50
Xfeat <- array(0, dim = c(B, k, 3)); ES <- matrix(NA, B, 6)
for (i in 1:B) {
  x <- as.numeric(Z_test[[i]])
  Xfeat[i,,1] <- x; Xfeat[i,,2] <- 1/x; Xfeat[i,,3] <- log(x)
  ES[i, ] <- c((1/(2*mean(x)))^2, min(x), median(x),
               sd(log(x)), mean(1/x), quantile(x, 0.10, names=FALSE))
}
tX <- torch_tensor(Xfeat, dtype=torch_float())$to(device=device)
tS <- torch_tensor(ES,    dtype=torch_float())$to(device=device)

preds <- matrix(NA, 3, B)
for (i in 1:3) {
  models[[i]]$eval()
  with_no_grad({ preds[i, ] <- as.numeric(models[[i]](tX, tS)$cpu()) })
}
```

```{r}
# Drop the dead member 1, average only 2 and 3
theta_nbe <- pmax(colMeans(preds[2:3, ]), 0)
summary(theta_nbe)

# Save
save(theta_mle, theta_nbe, file = "HW3_P2_estimates.RData")
write.csv(data.frame(MLE=theta_mle, NBE=theta_nbe), "HW3_P2.csv", row.names=FALSE)
```

**Training outcome.** Members 2 and 3 converged cleanly to final
validation wMSE of $0.0399$ and $0.0412$ respectively. Member 1 diverged
at epoch 30 and collapsed to a constant output, so it was dropped from
the ensemble and the final estimate averages only members 2 and 3.
The ensemble NBE has median $0.0103$ and mean $0.320$, noticeably more
aggressive at the boundary than the MLE (quartiles $\approx 20\times$
smaller) while still tracking the upper range.

#### Part 3 — MCMC

We run a random-walk Metropolis–Hastings sampler with log-scale proposals
$\log\theta^\star = \log\theta^{(t)}+\epsilon,\,\epsilon\sim N(0,\sigma^2)$,
$\sigma=0.3$, using an $\text{Exp}(\text{rate}=3)$ prior (matching the
continuous part of the NBE training prior). The log-posterior inner loop
is implemented in C++ via `Rcpp` for speed — the full sweep over all
1000 datasets takes $\approx 4$ seconds total. For each chain we run
20{,}000 iterations with 5{,}000 burn-in and report the posterior mean.


```{r}
library(Rcpp)

cppFunction('
NumericVector mh_sampler_cpp(NumericVector x, int n_iter, double sigma_prop,
                              double theta_init, double prior_rate) {
  int k = x.size();
  double sum_x = 0.0, sum_log_x = 0.0, sum_inv_x = 0.0;
  for (int i = 0; i < k; i++) {
    sum_x     += x[i];
    sum_log_x += log(x[i]);
    sum_inv_x += 1.0 / x[i];
  }
  double C = -0.5 * k * log(4.0 * M_PI) - 1.5 * sum_log_x - 0.25 * sum_inv_x;

  NumericVector chain(n_iter);
  chain[0] = theta_init;

  double log_post_cur = C - theta_init * sum_x + k * sqrt(theta_init)
                         + log(prior_rate) - prior_rate * theta_init;

  for (int t = 1; t < n_iter; t++) {
    double theta_cur = chain[t - 1];
    double log_theta_star = log(theta_cur) + R::rnorm(0.0, sigma_prop);
    double theta_star = exp(log_theta_star);

    double log_post_star = C - theta_star * sum_x + k * sqrt(theta_star)
                             + log(prior_rate) - prior_rate * theta_star;

    // Jacobian for log-transform proposal
    double log_alpha = log_post_star - log_post_cur +
                       log(theta_star) - log(theta_cur);

    if (log(R::runif(0.0, 1.0)) < log_alpha) {
      chain[t]    = theta_star;
      log_post_cur = log_post_star;
    } else {
      chain[t] = theta_cur;
    }
  }
  return chain;
}
')

# R wrapper
mcmc_sample <- function(x, n_iter = 20000, burn = 5000,
                         theta_init = NULL, sigma_prop = 0.3,
                         prior_rate = 3) {
  if (is.null(theta_init)) theta_init <- max((1/(2*mean(x)))^2, 1e-6)
  chain <- mh_sampler_cpp(x, n_iter, sigma_prop, theta_init, prior_rate)
  list(chain = chain, burn = burn,
       post = chain[(burn + 1):n_iter])
}
```


```{r}
res1 <- mcmc_sample(Z_test[[1]], n_iter = 30000, burn = 5000, sigma_prop = 0.3)
chain_post <- res1$post

cat("Posterior mean:  ", round(mean(chain_post), 5), "\n")
cat("Posterior median:", round(median(chain_post), 5), "\n")
cat("95% CI:          [",
    round(quantile(chain_post, 0.025), 5), ",",
    round(quantile(chain_post, 0.975), 5), "]\n")
cat("ESS:             ", round(coda::effectiveSize(chain_post), 1), "\n")

par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(chain_post, type = "l", col = "grey40",
     main = "Trace plot (post burn-in)",
     xlab = "Iteration", ylab = expression(theta))
abline(h = mean(chain_post), col = "red", lwd = 2)

hist(chain_post, breaks = 50, col = "steelblue", border = "white",
     probability = TRUE, main = "Posterior of Theta", xlab = expression(theta))
abline(v = mean(chain_post), col = "red", lwd = 2)
abline(v = quantile(chain_post, c(0.025, 0.975)), col = "blue", lty = 2)

acf(chain_post, lag.max = 100, main = "Autocorrelation")

rm_vals <- cumsum(chain_post) / seq_along(chain_post)
plot(rm_vals, type = "l", col = "steelblue",
     main = "Running mean", xlab = "Iteration", ylab = expression(bar(theta)))
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
```
```{r}
theta_mcmc <- numeric(length(Z_test))

t0 <- Sys.time()
for (i in seq_along(Z_test)) {
  res <- mcmc_sample(Z_test[[i]], n_iter = 20000, burn = 5000, sigma_prop = 0.3)
  theta_mcmc[i] <- mean(res$post)
  if (i %% 100 == 0) {
    cat("Done", i, "/", length(Z_test),
        " elapsed:", round(as.numeric(Sys.time() - t0, units = "secs"), 1), "s\n")
  }
}

cat("\nSummary of MCMC posterior means:\n")
summary(theta_mcmc)
```

**Convergence for dataset 1.** Posterior mean $0.01133$, median $0.00979$,
95% credible interval $[0.0010,\,0.0304]$, effective sample size $\approx 800$
(out of $25{,}000$ post-burn-in draws). The trace plot shows good mixing
with no drift, the autocorrelation decays to zero by lag $\sim 60$, and
the running mean stabilizes after $\sim 15{,}000$ iterations. All
diagnostics indicate the chain has converged.

```{r}
save(theta_mle, theta_nbe, theta_mcmc, file = "HW3_P2_estimates.RData")
write.csv(data.frame(MLE = theta_mle, NBE = theta_nbe, MCMC = theta_mcmc),
          "HW3_P2.csv", row.names = FALSE)
```


#### Uncertainty Quantification for Test Dataset 1

**MCMC uncertainty** is reported as the posterior mean, 95% credible
interval, and posterior standard deviation from the chain above.

**NBE uncertainty** is quantified via nonparametric bootstrap: we resample
the 50 observations with replacement $B=1000$ times, re-compute the
enriched features and expert summaries for each resample, and pass each
through the trained ensemble to obtain a distribution of point estimates.

```{r}
# -----------------------------------------------------------------
# Non-parametric bootstrap for NBE uncertainty on test dataset 1
# -----------------------------------------------------------------
B        <- 1000
k        <- 50
x1       <- as.numeric(Z_test[[1]])

# Build bootstrap sample tensor: B datasets, each of size k,
# each resampled with replacement from x1
set.seed(2026)
boot_idx <- matrix(sample(1:k, B * k, replace = TRUE), nrow = B, ncol = k)

# Construct enriched features and expert stats for each bootstrap sample
Xboot <- array(0, dim = c(B, k, 3))
Sboot <- matrix(NA, B, 6)
for (b in 1:B) {
  xb <- x1[boot_idx[b, ]]
  Xboot[b, , 1] <- xb
  Xboot[b, , 2] <- 1 / xb
  Xboot[b, , 3] <- log(xb)
  Sboot[b, ] <- c(
    (1 / (2 * mean(xb)))^2,
    min(xb), median(xb),
    sd(log(xb)), mean(1 / xb),
    quantile(xb, 0.10, names = FALSE)
  )
}

tXb <- torch_tensor(Xboot, dtype = torch_float())$to(device = device)
tSb <- torch_tensor(Sboot, dtype = torch_float())$to(device = device)

# Predict from each ensemble member and average (use members 2 and 3)
boot_preds <- matrix(NA, 2, B)
ensemble_idx <- c(2, 3)
for (j in seq_along(ensemble_idx)) {
  i <- ensemble_idx[j]
  models[[i]]$eval()
  with_no_grad({
    boot_preds[j, ] <- as.numeric(models[[i]](tXb, tSb)$cpu())
  })
}
theta_boot <- pmax(colMeans(boot_preds), 0)

# Point estimate for dataset 1 (original, not bootstrapped)
theta_hat_1 <- theta_nbe[1]

# Summary
cat("NBE bootstrap results for test dataset 1:\n")
cat("  Point estimate:   ", round(theta_hat_1, 5), "\n")
cat("  Bootstrap mean:   ", round(mean(theta_boot), 5), "\n")
cat("  Bootstrap SD:     ", round(sd(theta_boot), 5), "\n")
cat("  95% bootstrap CI: [",
    round(quantile(theta_boot, 0.025), 5), ",",
    round(quantile(theta_boot, 0.975), 5), "]\n")

# Histogram
hist(theta_boot, breaks = 40, col = "coral", border = "white",
     main = "NBE Bootstrap Distribution (Test Dataset 1)",
     xlab = expression(hat(theta)))
abline(v = theta_hat_1, col = "red", lwd = 2)
abline(v = quantile(theta_boot, c(0.025, 0.975)), col = "blue", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
legend("topright",
       legend = c("Point estimate", "95% CI"),
       col = c("red", "blue"), lty = c(1, 2), lwd = 2, bty = "n")
```



```{r}
# MCMC results (from earlier chunk)
mcmc_mean <- mean(chain_post)
mcmc_ci   <- quantile(chain_post, c(0.025, 0.975))
mcmc_sd   <- sd(chain_post)

# NBE bootstrap results
nbe_mean  <- mean(theta_boot)
nbe_ci    <- quantile(theta_boot, c(0.025, 0.975))
nbe_sd    <- sd(theta_boot)

comparison <- data.frame(
  Method     = c("MCMC", "NBE bootstrap"),
  PointEst   = c(round(mcmc_mean, 5), round(theta_hat_1, 5)),
  SD         = c(round(mcmc_sd, 5),   round(nbe_sd, 5)),
  CI_lower   = c(round(mcmc_ci[1], 5), round(nbe_ci[1], 5)),
  CI_upper   = c(round(mcmc_ci[2], 5), round(nbe_ci[2], 5)),
  CI_width   = c(round(diff(mcmc_ci), 5), round(diff(nbe_ci), 5))
)

print(comparison, row.names = FALSE)

# Overlay plot
hist(chain_post, breaks = 50, col = "steelblue", border = "white",
     probability = TRUE,
     main = "MCMC posterior", xlab = expression(theta),
     xlim = range(c(chain_post, theta_boot)))
abline(v = mcmc_mean, col = "red", lwd = 2)
abline(v = mcmc_ci,  col = "darkblue", lty = 2, lwd = 2)

hist(theta_boot, breaks = 50, col = "coral", border = "white",
     probability = TRUE,
     main = "NBE bootstrap", xlab = expression(theta),
     xlim = range(c(chain_post, theta_boot)))
abline(v = theta_hat_1, col = "red", lwd = 2)
abline(v = nbe_ci, col = "darkblue", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
```

#### Comparison and Discussion

| Method | Dataset 1 point est. | 95% interval | SD |
|---|---|---|---|
| MCMC | $0.01133$ | $[0.0010,\,0.0304]$ | $0.00780$ |
| NBE (bootstrap) | $0.00074$ | $[0.000,\,0.0413]$ | $0.01149$ |

**Interpretation.** The two methods quantify different notions of
uncertainty. The MCMC credible interval reflects posterior belief about
$\theta$ given this specific dataset under an $\text{Exp}(3)$ prior.
The NBE bootstrap distribution instead reflects sampling variability of
the trained estimator under resampling of the observed data.
Both intervals contain the MCMC point estimate, confirming the methods
are consistent. The NBE bootstrap has a sharp spike at zero with a long
right tail — the characteristic shape of an estimator trained under a
weighted loss that aggressively rewards predicting $\theta=0$ when the
data look boundary-like.

**Summary across 1000 datasets.**

| Statistic | MLE | NBE | MCMC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Min | $0.000$ | $0.000$ | $0.000$ |
| Median | $0.0162$ | $0.0103$ | $0.0230$ |
| Mean | $0.3515$ | $0.3199$ | $0.2924$ |
| Max | $3.2224$ | $2.1990$ | $2.2757$ |

All three methods agree on the overall shape of the estimated
distribution, but differ systematically near the boundary. The NBE is
most aggressive at pushing small estimates toward zero (lower 1st
quartile), which should yield the smallest weighted MSE under the
scoring metric $w(\theta)=1/(\theta+0.001)$ since errors at small
$\theta$ dominate the total score. MCMC is the most conservative near
zero because the Exp(3) prior pulls small posteriors upward. The
plain MLE has no shrinkage toward zero and is expected to perform
worst under the weighted metric. On validation data the NBE ensemble
achieved a weighted MSE of approximately $0.04$ — about two orders of
magnitude better than the MLE on the same simulated holdout.
